name: Type Diagnostics — Origin Tracking, Ambiguity, Rendering, Layered Explanations
summary: A type checker spends much of its observable lifetime explaining itself. Origin tracking through inference and constraint solving; ambiguity versus definite mismatch; rendering of internal types into human-readable form; concise messages for experienced users versus expanded explanations for learners.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
A type checker spends much of its observable lifetime explaining itself. The distinguishing axis across diagnostic designs is *how much origin and explanation context flows alongside each generated constraint*: solver-shaped messages (the obligation that failed, with no source story) versus user-shaped messages (the source positions and language-level concepts the programmer typed).

Production checkers invest heavily in origin tracking, ambiguity classification, and human-readable rendering. The cost is solver-architecture overhead; the benefit is that error messages stop being a barrier to language adoption.

sections.title: Origin tracking
Ambiguity and defaulting
sections.content:
Ambiguity is not the same as type error. Numeric literals, overloaded functions, implicit instances, generic associated types, and effect variables may remain unsolved without additional context. A language needs a defaulting policy or a requirement for annotations.

Defaulting is convenient for ergonomics but introduces non-locality — adding an unrelated declaration can change which default fires. The constraint-based architecture (`data/constraint-based-typing`) makes the choice *explicit* ("this constraint defaulted to X because no other resolution was found") rather than buried in the inference algorithm.

sections.title: Human-readable type rendering
sections.content:
Internal type representations are often unsuitable for users. The diagnostic renderer should simplify aliases, hide inference-variable internals, choose stable names, avoid exposing normalized projections unless necessary, and show enough structure to explain the problem.

The rendering algorithm matters. Naive rendering of a row-polymorphic effect type or a deeply-nested conditional type quickly becomes incomprehensible; production checkers (TypeScript, GHC, Scala, Rust) maintain dedicated rendering passes that prefer source-level vocabulary, hide internal flags, and progressively disclose detail as the user requests it (e.g., `--explain` flags or hover-on-type).

sections.title: Teaching mode and expert mode
sections.content:
A language can support layered diagnostics: concise messages by default, expanded explanations on request, and machine-readable JSON for tools. Type errors are often where users learn the language, so the checker should preserve a clear story about how the error follows from the rules.

Elm and Roc are notable for investing in beginner-friendly diagnostics; rustc's `--error-explain` system maps each error code to a long-form explanation; TypeScript's quick-fix UI is a primary diagnostics surface for many programmers. The lesson for language designers is that **diagnostics quality is part of the language design, not a tooling afterthought** — programmers experience the type system through error messages more than through specifications.
